fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Your primary international diversification engine. S&P 500 gives exposure to 500 of the world's largest companies (Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia etc.) — businesses that are simply not available on Indian exchanges. The quantitative case is hard to argue with: Sharpe ratio of 2.65 is the highest in the entire universe, meaning you get the most return per unit of risk — exactly what a Sharpe-maximiser wants. Beta vs Nifty 50 is just 0.07, meaning it moves almost completely independently of Indian markets — this dramatically reduces portfolio-level correlation and improves the overall risk-adjusted return of the combined portfolio. Yearly fee: 0.20% — near-zero cost. Tax: equity-taxed (≥65% equity). LTCG 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25L after 1 year.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential NASDAQ 100 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: A high-octane satellite to the S&P 500 core. NASDAQ 100 is concentrated in the world's most innovative technology companies — higher growth potential but also higher volatility (21% vs 16% for S&P 500). Sharpe of 2.14 is still exceptionally strong. Beta vs Indian market is 0.16 — still near-zero correlation with Nifty, adding further diversification. Combined with the S&P 500 allocation, your total international exposure hits 30%, which meaningfully decouples your wealth from India-specific risk events. Yearly fee: 0.20%. Tax: equity-taxed.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: India's mid-cap segment has historically delivered the best long-term returns among domestic equity categories. This index fund captures the Nifty Midcap 150 — the 150 companies just below the top 50 — at a yearly fee of just 0.20%, which is the same cost as an active fund's management overhead. 5-year CAGR of 18.7%, Sharpe of 1.00. Critically, this is the passive, low-cost way to get mid-cap exposure rather than paying 1.15% to an active manager. Fee comparison: over 18 years, the 0.95% fee saving vs active mid-cap funds compounds to roughly 18-20% more corpus for identical gross returns. Equity-taxed.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: This is the one active mid-cap fund that justifies its 1.15% fee — and here's the math: 3-year CAGR of 26.56% vs the index fund's 23.19% — an active outperformance (alpha) of +13.73% annualised, Sharpe of 1.12. After subtracting the 0.95% fee premium over the index, net alpha still stands at ~12.8% over 3 years. A 30-year fund house with ₹8,172 Cr AUM providing institutional-grade liquidity. It is held alongside the index fund deliberately — if the active fund's alpha degrades over time (which you should check annually), the index fund already provides the passive beta. Equity-taxed.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Highest domestic 3-year CAGR in the small-cap category at 31.33%, with a 5-year CAGR of 23.71%. Small-cap is the highest expected-return domestic asset class over 18-year horizons — essential for hitting the ₹12 Cr target. The Sharpe of 0.59 looks lower because small-cap is inherently volatile, but the absolute return per unit of downside is superior over long periods. Beta of 0.92 vs Nifty shows controlled market sensitivity. This is SIP-only — small cap is NOT suitable for a lumpsum deployment due to valuation concentration risk. Equity-taxed. Critical warning: this fund can drop 40-50% in a severe bear market — you will not sell.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: The portfolio's volatility dampener and a second international diversification layer. PPFAS holds ~25-35% in overseas stocks (Google, Meta, Amazon etc.) directly in its portfolio, which is unique among Indian flexi-cap funds. The data tells the story: volatility of just 10.5% vs the category average of ~16%, and beta of 0.55 vs Nifty — meaning when Indian markets drop 10%, this fund typically drops only ~5.5%. Alpha of 11.6% shows genuine stock-picking skill. The ₹88,404 Cr AUM is the largest in the category — institutional stability. This fund is the shock absorber that prevents your portfolio Sharpe from collapsing during Indian market downturns. Equity-taxed.

fund.name: WhiteOak Capital ELSS Tax Saver Fund Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: Pure tax arbitrage play. As a 30% slab investor, ₹1.5L/year invested here saves ₹46,800 in income tax annually under Section 80C — that's an immediate 31.2% guaranteed return on the first ₹1.5L deployed, before the fund even does anything. Over 18 years, if you reinvest those annual tax savings of ₹46,800, at even 12% CAGR, they compound to an additional ~₹34L. WhiteOak was chosen over older ELSS funds: 3-year CAGR of 20.95%, lowest tracking error (6.18%) in the ELSS universe — meaning it's genuinely active, not a closet index fund in disguise. Low beta of 0.93 keeps drawdowns manageable. 3-year lock-in per SIP instalment. Equity-taxed post lock-in, with 80C benefit on entry.

fund.name: UTI Nifty 500 Value 50 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Tactical factor-tilt satellite position. This tracks the Nifty 500 Value 50 — 50 deep-value stocks from the broader Nifty 500, a factor (value) that has academically demonstrated long-run outperformance over cap-weighted indices. The 3-year CAGR of 31.14% and Sharpe of 1.48 at just 0.20% yearly fee is extraordinary. Small allocation only (part of the 'other' category) due to shorter track record (2y), kept as a diversifying factor bet. This is the quant investor's edge: factor exposure via low-cost passive — not active manager risk.

plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction = 0.7: The equity allocation consists of 70% passive/index funds (S&P 500 Index, NASDAQ 100 Index, Nifty Midcap 150 Index, Nifty 500 Value 50 Index) and 30% active funds (Nippon Mid Cap, Bandhan Small Cap, Parag Parikh Flexi Cap, WhiteOak ELSS). Only equity funds are considered; debt/gold/hybrid not applicable. Thus, 70% of equity sleeve is passive.

turnover_score = 0.9: The plan lacks explicit rebalancing guidelines or review checkpoints. Allocation appears static with long-term SIP commitments and lumpsum entries. No mention of tactical shifts, sector rotation, or frequent rebalancing. Implies a buy-and-hold orientation with minimal turnover.

cost_emphasis_score = 0.8: Expense ratios are explicitly discussed, especially the cost advantage of index funds (e.g., 0.2% vs 1.15%). The rationale highlights fee savings compounding over 18 years. However, several active funds with higher expense ratios (1.0–1.2%) are included based on performance, indicating cost is important but not the sole criterion.

research_vs_cost_score = 0.6: The plan references quantitative metrics (Sharpe, beta, alpha, tracking error) and uses them to justify active selections (e.g., Parag Parikh, WhiteOak ELSS). However, it avoids deep qualitative analysis (e.g., management quality, moats). Passive funds are selected based on factor exposure and cost, not individual stock research. Balance between metrics-driven and cost-aware selection.

time_horizon_alignment_score = 0.9: The plan references an 18-year horizon, SIP-only for volatile assets (small cap), and compounding of tax savings over decades. Strategy is clearly designed for long-term wealth creation with infrequent intervention.

portfolio_activeness_score = 0.65: Passive funds dominate (70% of equity), with low expense ratios and index-based mandates. However, active funds like Bandhan Small Cap (tracking error 11.72%, alpha 9.78), Nippon Mid Cap (TE 8.59%, alpha 13.73), and Parag Parikh (beta 0.55, active international holdings) show genuine active management. WhiteOak ELSS has low tracking error (6.18%) but is still active. Portfolio-weighted, the mix leans passive but includes meaningful active bets with rationale. Risk metrics support this blended profile.

pqs.reasoning:
The investment plan is exceptionally well-aligned with Sheldon Cooper's aggressive risk appetite, 18-year horizon, and Sharpe-ratio-maximising objective. The portfolio is heavily equity-oriented (100% equity exposure), with deliberate international diversification (S&P 500, NASDAQ 100, and PPFAS's overseas holdings), mid/small-cap tilt for higher return potential, and factor-based exposure (value via Nifty 500 Value 50), all justified with quantitative metrics like Sharpe ratio, alpha, beta, and tracking error — directly addressing the investor's data-driven preferences.

Diversification is strong across geographies (US and India), market caps (large, mid, small), and fund types (index, active, flexi-cap, ELSS). The inclusion of both passive (low-cost index) and selective active funds (justified by alpha net of fees) shows a nuanced approach. The only minor gap is the lack of explicit sectoral or bond exposure, but given the aggressive mandate and long horizon, this is reasonable.

Risk-return is appropriate: high volatility assets (small-cap, NASDAQ) are included but balanced with lower-beta stabilisers like PPFAS Flexi Cap. The plan acknowledges downside risks (e.g., 40-50% drawdown warning for small-cap) and uses SIPs for volatile segments, showing risk awareness.

Internal consistency is high: every fund rationale ties back to the investor’s goals (e.g., Sharpe maximisation, tax savings, international exposure). Allocation modes (SIP vs lumpsum) are logically assigned based on risk (e.g., SIP-only for small-cap). The 100% equity allocation is coherent with the aggressive risk profile.

Tax efficiency is optimal: the investor is in the 30% tax slab, and the plan fully utilises 80C by allocating 10% to an ELSS fund, correctly highlighting the immediate 31.2% tax-saving return. All funds are equity-taxed (≥65% equity), avoiding slab-taxed debt funds that would be inefficient for this investor. The rationale explicitly discusses tax treatment for each relevant fund, including post-lock-in equity taxation. No ELSS is recommended beyond 80C headroom, and no slab-taxed instruments are used — perfect tax-awareness.
